commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=7445e882348fd3832ac40ce71b253579aac93b00)
commands.review_summary:
Adds a cron service validator and regression test requiring recurring agentTurn cron jobs to set an explicit model, justify premium or persistent-session usage, and use lightContext for script-runner prompts.

Reproducibility: yes. for the review findings: source inspection shows plugin cron scheduled turns and documented recurring isolated jobs omit fields the new guard requires. I did not execute tests because this review is read-only.
commands.review_followup: Needs maintainer policy review and contributor proof because the fail-closed model requirement changes documented cron behavior and plugin SDK scheduling semantics.

target.title: feat(cron): guard recurring agentTurn jobs against unsafe cost defaults
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a runtime cost guardrail for recurring `agentTurn` cron jobs. Validates at `createJob` and `applyJobPatch` time so unsafe configurations fail loudly instead of quietly burning premium tokens.

Recurring (`schedule.kind = "every" | "cron"`) `agentTurn` jobs now must:

- set `payload.model` explicitly (no inherited premium default)
- carry a justification phrase in `description` when using a premium model (`opus`, `claude-4`, `gpt-5.4` non-mini/non-nano, `gpt-5.5` non-mini/non-nano)
- carry a justification phrase when using a persistent `session:*` target
- set `payload.lightContext = true` when the message looks like a script/command runner (`run|exec|execute` + `python3?|bash|node|npm|pnpm|yarn|openclaw|scripts/`)

Accepted justification phrases (case-insensitive regex): `cron-cost-allow`, `premium justified`, `persistent justified`, `budget justified`.

## Motivation (real incident)

In a real deployed OpenClaw setup, an inherited-premium recurring `thread-watchdog` cron at every-10-minutes burned roughly $0.92 per no-op run (~146k tokens of inherited context, Opus-class model, persistent session growing over time, silent `NO_REPLY` output). One week of that pattern accumulated ~$1,230 of automation spend with zero user value.

Aggregate evidence from that real setup, week of May 4-10:
- ~788.5M tokens, ~$2,146 estimated spend
- `thread-watchdog`-shaped usage: ~$1,230.75, ~213.7M tokens, ~1,997 calls
- Single no-op trace, May 6 08:09 UTC: exec script → `No stale threads.` → `NO_REPLY`; model `anthropic/claude-opus-4.7`; ~146,874 tokens; ~$0.92

## Real behavior proof (after-fix)

After applying the containment patch and this PR's runtime guardrail in a real OpenClaw deployment, the same `thread-watchdog` job now runs under the safe shape. Live `openclaw cron get` output for the affected job (redacted ids):

```
{
  "name": "thread-watchdog",
  "enabled": true,
  "schedule": { "kind": "every", "everyMs": 600000 },
  "sessionTarget": "isolated",
  "payload": {
    "kind": "agentTurn",
    "model": "openrouter/openai/gpt-5.4-nano",
    "thinking": "low",
    "lightContext": true,
    ...
  },
  "delivery": { "mode": "none" },
  "failureAlert": { "after": 1, "channel": "slack", "mode": "announce", ... },
  "state": {
    "lastRunStatus": "ok",
    "lastDurationMs": 6336,
    "consecutiveErrors": 0
  }
}
```

Spend impact, real setup:
- Before: ~$0.92/no-op run × 144 runs/day ≈ ~$132/day on this one cron
- After: nano + lightContext + isolated session ≈ pennies/day

The guardrail in this PR makes the *before* shape impossible to create or patch in the first place. Verified by attempting to add a recurring cron with an unset model, premium model without justification, and script-runner without lightContext, all rejected at create time.

## Design notes

- `PREMIUM_CRON_MODEL_MARKERS` is a named constant so the policy is one place to read, and downstream audit tooling can reference the same list.
- The premium list intentionally **excludes** mid-tier flagships like `moonshotai/kimi-k2-thinking`. Those sit ~10x nano cost, but two orders of magnitude below the actual budget killers. A daily kimi-tier cron is bounded; a recurring Opus cron is not. The guardrail is calibrated for the latter.
- "Premium marker AND cheap suffix" (e.g. `gpt-5.4-nano`, `gpt-5.4-mini`) is treated as not premium. Cheap variants of premium families are fine for recurring jobs.
- Operator precedence is no longer load-bearing: classification uses array `.some()` rather than a chain of `&&`/`||` so the next edit cannot accidentally invert the policy.

## Tests

`src/cron/service.issue-regressions.test.ts` — 11 passed.

New test `rejects unsafe recurring agentTurn cron jobs` covers each branch:
- unset model → rejected
- premium model without justification → rejected
- script-runner without lightContext → rejected
- safe nano script-runner with lightContext → accepted
- **policy lock-in: kimi-k2-thinking recurring without justification → accepted** (asserts mid-tier flagships are not classified as premium)

Existing tests touching this code path were updated to set `payload.model` explicitly (previously omitted) so the new guardrail does not break unrelated cases.

## Out of scope

This PR does not:
- introduce per-job budget caps or cost attribution
- audit or modify existing crons in any deployed runtime
- change cron scheduler behavior or persistence

Those are follow-ups.
target.author: joeyfrasier
